data.title: Hong Kong to open 4 new types of polling stations to boost Legco election turnout
data.description:
Hong Kong will introduce four new types of designated polling stations to make it easier for civil servants, medical workers, ethnic minority groups and elderly and disabled residents in care homes to vote in the Legislative Council election, while voting will be extended by two hours to 11.30 pm.
Announcing the moves on Thursday, David Lok Kai-hong, chairman of the Electoral Affairs Commission, said the new measures sought to address calls by society to help those working on election day who might be unable to reach their local polling stations in time.
To help the wider public, voting hours at all polling stations will also be extended, opening at 7.30am and closing at 11.30pm. Previously, voting was from 8.30am to 10.30pm.
Lok attributed the decision to extend opening times to the changing travel patterns of Hongkongers spending their holidays in mainland China.
On top of the 615 general polling stations, authorities would for the first time set up four new types of designated polling stations for the December 7 election, including 10 for civil servants and officers of disciplinary forces to allow them to vote and return to their posts as soon as possible, he said.

Seven polling stations will be set up near the city’s seven biggest hospitals to help medical workers cast their ballots, and another two in Wan Chai and Tsim Sha Tsui for voters attending religious activities at the Khalsa Diwan and the Kowloon Mosque and Islamic Centre.
Special outreach polling stations would also be set up at 11 care homes for the elderly and four centres for the disabled, Lok said, adding the election authorities would dispatch officers with ballot papers to the scene to help residents and care home workers vote.
The government has made an all-out push to boost the turnout of the Legco election – the second held under Beijing’s “patriots-only” overhaul, after the previous vote saw a record low participation rate of 30.2 per cent.
More to follow …

data.title: Chinese 3D-printed turbojet engine offers ‘new power option’ for military drones
data.description:
China has tested a 3D-printed turbojet engine in flight for the first time, a development that could support the country’s growing fleet of high-speed, low-cost and quick-to-produce military drones.
During the 30-minute flight on Sunday, the engine took a small, missile-shaped target drone to an altitude of 6km (3.8 miles) and a top speed of around 925km/h, about three quarters the speed of sound, state broadcaster CCTV reported on Thursday.
It said the engine operated normally throughout the mission, with no abnormalities in any of its parameters.
The engine – built entirely from 3D-printed rotor components and with more than 75 per cent of its total weight made from printed parts – produced about 160kg (350 pounds) of thrust and served as the drone’s sole source of propulsion during the test.
The aircraft was launched from a ground rail and recovered by parachute. CCTV did not disclose where the trial took place or the engine’s overall mass.
Mi Dong, from the institute that developed the engine under the state-owned Aero Engine Corporation of China, said the flight showed the system could operate reliably at higher altitudes and in more complex conditions, and that it was well matched to the aircraft using it.
It can “provide a new power option for platforms such as loitering munitions, unmanned aircraft and target drones, and has promising application prospects”, he told CCTV.
The use of 3D printing technology, mostly for individual components, started a decade ago.
Companies such as General Electric and Rolls-Royce were early adopters, using printed turbine blades, fuel nozzles and brackets to cut weight and simplify manufacturing.
Researchers and hobbyists in the US and Europe later built small, fully 3D-printed demonstration engines.

However, these prototypes were generally used for laboratory testing, and publicly reported examples of 3D-printed engines powering high-speed drones remain rare.
In July, the Chinese team conducted a captive flight test – where the payload craft does not separate from its carrier – in Inner Mongolia to observe how it performed in real flight conditions, finishing the test successfully and reaching an altitude of 4km.
CCTV said the engine’s design dramatically reduced the number of parts needed to build the engine, helping make it lighter and more efficient, and laying the groundwork for flights at higher altitudes and speeds.
The research team now plans to carry out more demanding flight tests to see how the engine performs in harsher environments and on a wider range of drones, Mi said.
He added that another priority was to refine the manufacturing process and prepare for larger-scale production. “We want to move from a technical success to a product success as quickly as possible,” he said.

data.title: Xi welcomes Thai king to China with pledge of progress for family-strength ties
data.description:
China stands ready to align development strategies with Thailand for “greater progress” in bilateral ties, President Xi Jinping told the visiting Thai monarch King Maha Vajiralongkorn in Beijing on Friday.
The Chinese leader described the two nations as “true good relatives, friends and partners” during his meeting with the king at the Great Hall of the People, according to the official broadcaster CCTV.
“China is willing to strengthen strategic alignment with Thailand,” Xi said, while pitching Beijing’s 15th five-year plan as offering potential synergies with the Southeast Asian nation’s development, CCTV reported.
Xi added that Beijing was ready to import more Thai agricultural products and work with Bangkok to “steadily” advance major infrastructure projects such as the China-Thailand railway, while broadening joint efforts on artificial intelligence, the digital economy and aerospace.
He also told the visiting king that China would actively support the Thai royal family’s charitable initiatives and enhance exchanges relating to sharing its poverty reduction experience.
The king described the two countries’ bilateral cooperation as “between brothers” and said that Thailand was ready to learn from China’s development experience, expand cooperation across various fields and enhance people-to-people exchanges, the Chinese release of the talks showed.

The state visit, which started on Thursday and will run through Monday, marks the first trip to China by a Thai monarch since the two nations established diplomatic ties half a century ago. It was hailed by both Beijing and Bangkok as a “historic” event.
The rare trip, which also coincides with the 50th anniversary of China-Thailand diplomatic relations – also praised by the two countries as a “golden jubilee” – is expected by observers to carry considerable significance for bilateral ties.
“Standing at a new historical starting point, I look forward to working with King Maha Vajiralongkorn to advance the China-Thailand community with a shared future and achieve greater progress in the next 50 years,” Xi said.
Before the meeting, Xi and his wife Peng Liyuan held a ceremony to welcome the king and Queen Suthida.
During the five-day trip, the king and queen will focus on institutions that highlight China’s development in science, technology, innovation and education, in addition to visiting cultural and religious sites in Beijing, according to Bangkok.
China has been Thailand’s largest trading partner for 12 straight years up to 2024. While Bangkok is one of two treaty partners with Washington in Southeast Asia, it has also deepened cooperation with Beijing in the security field, marked by a joint air force exercise in September.
King Maha Vajiralongkorn visited China four times as crown prince in 1987, 1988, 1992 and 1998. His most recent meeting with Xi was in Bangkok in 2022. His predecessor, the late King Bhumibol Adulyadej – who spent seven decades on the throne – did not visit China.
The Thai monarchy has played a significant role in sustaining Thai-Chinese relations through the work of the king’s younger sister.

Princess Maha Chakri Sirindhorn, who has studied Chinese art and language for the past 45 years, has been a frequent visitor to China and is officially regarded by Beijing as an “old friend of the Chinese people”.
In an interview with the official People’s Daily Online published on Thursday, China’s top envoy to Thailand, Zhang Jianwei, characterised the bilateral relationship as “at its best in history”.
Zhang added that China-Thailand relations went beyond the bilateral scope with “broad common interests and similar positions” in international and regional affairs, noting that Beijing supported a peaceful resolution of the Thai-Cambodian border dispute via the “Asean way”.
Simmering since a flare-up in July, tensions between Thailand and Cambodia reignited sharply this week, with Bangkok on Monday halting the Donald Trump administration-brokered ceasefire deal signed with Phnom Penh two weeks earlier.


Bangkok’s move followed a landmine blast that injured four Thai soldiers, followed two days later by reports from both sides of gunfire across the disputed boundary, with officials trading accusations of fresh clashes.
Asked about the recent situation, the Chinese foreign ministry said on Thursday that Beijing “sincerely hopes that both sides can exercise restraint”.
The king’s visit also comes as Bangkok works to ease Beijing’s concerns about Thailand’s safety for tourists following a spate of cases involving fraud syndicates and human trafficking, notably the kidnapping of Chinese actor Wang Xing earlier this year.
China is one of Thailand’s top sources of foreign visitors but in the first 10 months of this year, the number of inbound Chinese tourists fell almost 34 per cent compared with the same period last year, according to Thai government data.
Thailand also saw a 7.2 per cent decline in the total number of foreign tourist arrivals in the January-October period of 2025, compared with the same time frame last year.
Additional reporting by Maria Siow

data.title: National Games: Siobhan Haughey leads Hong Kong trio into 100 metres freestyle semis
data.description:
The fifth day of the National Games started with good news for Hong Kong swimmers, as Siobhan Haughey led a trio from the city who advanced to the 100 metres freestyle semi-finals.
Haughey, fresh from her gold medal in the 200m freestyle on Thursday night, moved comfortably into the semis on Friday, winning her heat in a time of 54.29 seconds, the fastest overall among the 45 swimmers.
Two other Hong Kong swimmers, 16-year-old Li Sum-yiu and 17-year-old Gilaine Ma, also qualified for the semi-finals, clocking 55.4 and 56.04.
The semi-finals are scheduled to take place at 7.07pm and 7.13pm.
Haughey’s biggest competitor could be Yang Junxuan, representing Shandong, who set China’s national record for this event during her starting leg for the 4x100m freestyle relay at the Paris Olympics, with a time of 52.48.

Haughey is still the Asian record-holder with 52.02 seconds, set two years ago in Germany.
She said on Thursday that her triumph in the 200m had given her “a lot of confidence for the upcoming Games”.
She has two more individual events to swim, the 50m freestyle and 50m breaststroke.
On the badminton court, the Hong Kong men’s team beat Beijing 3-0 to finish seventh.
Hong Kong were relegated to the placing bracket after finishing third in the group stage, missing a chance at the semi-finals.
Hong Kong player Angus Ng Ka-long, who had hoped for a medal, on Wednesday posted an emotional message on social media, describing his disappointment as a “forever regret” and stating that he “needed some time to digest” the outcome.
The finals for both the men’s and women’s team badminton will be contested between Zhejiang and Jiangsu. The women’s final was scheduled to start at 2pm, while the men’s final was scheduled for 7pm.
Three equestrian riders, Su Yuxuan, Thomas Ho and Nicole Pearson, resumed after being placed 16th, 22nd and 24th after the opening dressage discipline on Thursday.
In Friday’s cross-country riding phase, Su improved his standing to 10th place, while Ho claimed 20th position. The 58-year-old rider Pearson, however, was eliminated from the competition after falling from her horse.
Both Su and Ho will proceed to Saturday’s final jumping competition.
At the Hong Kong Velodrome, three track cycling gold medals will be awarded on Friday: women’s and men’s team pursuit and women’s 250m time trial.
Hong Kong Golf Club in Fanling was hosting the second round of individual and team golf competition.

data.title: ONE Championship: Superlek Kiatmoo9, Yuki Yoza trade verbal kicks ahead of Tokyo showdown
data.description:
Superlek Kiatmoo9 has seen and heard it all in his storied career, but Japan’s Yuki Yoza may have given him extra motivation ahead of their clash at ONE 173 in Tokyo.
The Thai superstar, known worldwide as “The Kicking Machine”, makes his return to kickboxing this weekend after a brief injury lay-off. His opponent has been making headlines of his own, confidently declaring that he will be the new Kicking Machine after their bout.
Either way, many fans have earmarked this fight as the pick of the bunch on a card full of stone cold bangers, so if it lives up to the hype, the viewers will be in for something very special.
Superlek was unmoved by Yoza’s bravado. “I think nothing of it,” he said with a smile. “Maybe he talks so much because he’s scared of me. This is my comeback, and I’m very focused.
“Yuki talks a lot, but I’ll see him on Sunday.”


The 30-year-old has competed hundreds of times, collected world titles and remained one of the most respected names in Muay Thai and kickboxing. Yet even with his accolades, Superlek admitted that returning to Japan for a major card had him fired up again.
“I’m very excited,” he said. “It’s been a while, and this is a big event. I’m fighting a Japanese superstar, but I’m a superstar too. I’ll remind everyone of that.”
Yoza, 27, who fights out of the Vasilius Gym alongside Masaaki Noiri and Takeru Segawa – who also have monster clashes on the card – enters with the confidence of a man on a winning streak and the support of a home crowd.

After facing Superlek at the official face-off, he admitted he could sense the Thai’s intensity. “When I looked into his eyes, I could feel his strength,” he said. “That made me even more excited for this fight.”
Asked if he feared Superlek’s trademark low kicks, which famously damaged Takeru’s leg last year, Yoza dismissed the concern.
“He’s strong, but I’ve trained with 100kg fighters in Kyokushin karate. I can handle the kicks. I’ve taken harder,” he said.
The Japanese star also vowed to push the pace in Tokyo. “I’ll show all my weapons,” he said. “I don’t plan to fight the same way he does. I want to put on a show and go for the knockout.”


Superlek, meanwhile, said he had added new tricks to his arsenal since his last outing. “It might be the same as before, but with new elements,” he teased. “I’ve trained hard. I’m ready to show what’s next.”
Both men have mutual respect despite the verbal exchanges. Yoza spoke positively of his opponent, calling him “a true superstar” and said he expected a “beautiful fight for the fans”.
But he also made it clear he saw this as his moment to break through. “It’s not the toughest fight of my career,” Yoza said. “I had a harder one before. This is my chance to climb the rankings and move towards the world title.”
Superlek echoed that sentiment, seeing Yoza’s rise as a reflection of Japan’s growing influence in striking.
“There are many Thai versus Japan fights on this card,” he said. “I hope all the Thai athletes show their best skills and remind everyone that Thailand still has the best strikers in the world.”

data.title: Tamarind is ugly but delicious. How to use the sour, sweet fruit to make Thai crunchy fish
data.description:
Tamarind is a rather ugly fruit; in its natural state, the thin-shelled pod resembles old, dust-covered dog droppings. The taste, though, is anything but dry and dusty; it is sour and sweet.
It is used as a seasoning in Africa, Southeast Asia and India, where it is usually available fresh, as a paste and as a thick, dark syrup.
When buying fresh tamarind, look for pods that are swollen and full, rather than thin and flat, as this means they are ripe and therefore sweeter and less acidic.
For tamarind paste, cut or break off the appropriate amount and stir it into hot water to dissolve it, then strain it to remove the fibres and seeds.
Tamarind is also made into a chewy candy and, when diluted with cold water, makes a refreshing drink.
Tamarind is used in the traditional version of pad Thai, where its sour flavour is balanced with palm sugar (cheaper restaurants rely primarily on tomato ketchup as the main seasoning).

In Malaysia, any dish with the word “assam” in it is sure to include tamarind. One of my favourite Malaysian dishes is assam laksa, which is not easy to make.
It requires fish stock; a spice paste made with chillies, shallots and belacan (fermented shrimp paste); tamarind water; an oily fresh fish such as mackerel; and garnishes that include fresh herbs, chillies, fresh pineapple and dark, pungent shrimp paste.
Thai crunchy fish
Tass, our Thai helper, makes variations on crunchy fish for every dinner party we have, and invariably, there is nothing left on the serving platter.

Tass likes to use white sea bass (which is in the croaker family), but says you can choose another firm, mild-tasting fish. Do not use an oily variety, such as mackerel or salmon.
The thinly sliced fish is fried at a relatively low temperature for quite a long time – about 10-15 minutes – until most of the moisture is gone, making the pieces firm and crunchy. It is then coated in a sticky, tamarind-based sauce.
Gogi (or Kogi) powder is Thai tempura flour that makes a good crunchy coating for seafood and meat.
If you cannot find it (although it is worth seeking out), use plain (all-purpose) flour.

Ingredients

650 grams (23 oz) skin-on fish fillets


15ml (1 tbsp) soy sauce, preferably Thai soy sauce


1 tsp finely ground white pepper


6 large shallots, peeled


5-7 red bird’s-eye chillies


3-4 spring onions


20ml (scant ¾ oz) tamarind paste (without seeds)


15ml (1 tbsp) fish sauce


15 grams (1 tbsp) granulated sugar


75 grams (2⅔ oz) cashews


Gogi/Kogi powder, as necessary


Cooking oil, as necessary

1 Thinly slice the fish fillets – the pieces should be no thicker than 5mm (¼ in). Put the pieces on a plate, then add the soy sauce and white pepper and mix well.
2 Halve the shallots, then thinly slice them. Slice the chillies into thin rounds, and mince the spring onions.
3 Mix the tamarind paste with 20ml (1 tbsp and 1 tsp) of hot water and stir well. Mix in the fish sauce and sugar.
4 Pour oil to the depth of about 5cm (2 in) into a wok and heat to 160 degrees Celsius (320 degrees Fahrenheit) over a medium flame. Add the cashews and fry for about a minute, or until pale golden. Use a flat mesh sieve to lift the cashews out of the oil, then drain them on paper towels.
5 Add the shallots to the oil and fry for about three to four minutes, or until golden to medium brown, stirring often. Turn off the flame, then lift the shallots out of the oil using the flat mesh sieve. Place over a bowl to drain for a few minutes, then transfer to a bowl.

6 Sprinkle about 60 grams (½ cup) of Gogi/Kogi powder over the fish slices and mix so they are lightly but evenly coated. Shake off the excess powder.
7 Reheat the oil to 160 degrees Celsius (320 degrees Fahrenheit). Add all the fish to the oil. At first, it will seem like too much fish – it will not be submerged in the oil. Leave undisturbed for about 30 seconds, then use a wok spatula to carefully scoop under the pile of fish and gently lift and turn it. Do not stir-fry vigorously, or the fish might fall apart.
8 Again, leave the fish undisturbed for about 30 seconds before lifting and turning it. Repeat the process of lifting and turning the fish, then leaving it undisturbed, several more times.
9 As the fish loses moisture, it will shrink, and towards the end of the frying process, will be mostly submerged in the oil. The fish takes about 13 minutes to fry. Lift and stir the fish more frequently, starting at the halfway point, and towards the end, stir it almost constantly. Do not rush the process, and adjust the flame as necessary so it does not fry too quickly. When it is ready, the fish will be deep golden, and when you snap a piece in half, it will be cooked all the way through. Drain the fish in a metal sieve.

10 Pour off almost all the oil. Place the wok (no need to wash it) over a low-medium flame. Add the tamarind mixture and stir almost constantly, simmering it until it is a light syrup consistency.
11 Add the chillies and stir constantly for about 15 seconds, then add the fried shallots. Turn off the flame and stir until the shallots are lightly coated, then mix in half the spring onion.
12 Add the fish and cashews into the wok and mix well so the ingredients are evenly distributed. Transfer to a serving platter and scatter with the remaining spring onion. Serve immediately.
Food styling: Nellie Ming Lee. Kitchen: courtesy of Junk Kitchen
This content first appeared in Post Magazine in 2014 and 2022.

data.title: ‘Massive’ Russian aerial attack hits nearly every district of Ukraine’s capital Kyiv
data.description:
Almost every district in Kyiv came under “massive” attack on Friday morning, the Ukrainian capital’s mayor said, with one death reported and 30 residential buildings damaged across the city.
Russia, which launched a full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, has in recent months intensified its attacks on infrastructure, particularly targeting Ukrainian energy facilities and rail systems, as well as residential areas.
Missiles and drones were targeting critical infrastructure in the capital on Friday, said Mykola Kalashnyk, the head of the Kyiv regional military administration.
Mayor Vitaly Klitschko called it a “massive enemy attack”, saying air defence forces were in operation.

An elderly woman was killed and at least 24 people, including a pregnant woman and a 10-year-old boy, were wounded in the attack that damaged 30 residential buildings in nine districts, Kyiv police said.
Ukrainian emergency services rescued more than 40 people from fires and destruction across the city.
“Sections of heating networks were damaged,” Klitschko wrote on Telegram, with some buildings in northeastern Desnyansky district temporarily left without heat.
Electricity and water supplies could also be disrupted, he added.
Transport was partially blocked, city administration authorities said, with buses and trams delayed.
Tracer bullets were used against drones and several anti-missile systems were deployed.

“Russians are hitting residential buildings. There are a lot of damaged high-rise buildings throughout Kyiv, almost in every district,” Tymur Tkachenko, the head of the city’s military administration, wrote on social media.
The attack comes as Kyiv’s Western allies ratchet up pressure on Russia.
On Wednesday, Canada unveiled new sanctions targeting Russia’s drone and energy production, as well as infrastructure used to launch cyberattacks.
G7 foreign ministers that day called for an immediate ceasefire in Ukraine, voicing “unwavering” support for the country’s territorial integrity.
And the European Commission is considering using part of Russia’s assets frozen after its invasion to provide Kyiv with a loan for budgetary and military support over the next two years.
But after almost four years of war, both sides are heavily entrenched with Moscow rejecting ceasefire calls and efforts by US President Donald Trump to revive a long-stalled peace deal.

Russian forces have been grinding across eastern Ukraine for months, trying to take control of the Donetsk and Luhansk regions.
Russia said Monday it had captured three more villages along the sprawling front line, where it is pressing its advantage in manpower and equipment.
Experts say Russia’s latest strikes on energy infrastructure are putting Ukraine at risk of heating outages ahead of the winter months.
Ukraine has in turn stepped up attacks on Russian infrastructure, and sought to strike further beyond the front.
Russian forces downed more than 200 Ukrainian drones overnight into Friday, Moscow’s defence ministry said, including 66 over Krasnodar Krai and 45 over Saratov in the south.
A fire broke out at the Sheskharis oil refinery, one of the largest in Russia, in the Black Sea port of Novorossiysk, before being brought under control, Krasnodar Krai authorities said.
A civilian vessel there was also damaged, with three of the crew wounded and hospitalised, they added.

data.title: Malaysian economy’s hot run continues despite Trump’s tariff squeeze
data.description:
Malaysia predicts growth this year is likely to approach 4.8 per cent after a resurgent exports sector defied the impact of US tariffs in the third quarter.
The nation’s growth rate in 2025 is set to be “closer to” the upper range of the official forecast of 4 per cent to 4.8 per cent, and may even exceed it, Bank Negara Malaysia Governor Abdul Rasheed Ghaffour said at a briefing in Kuala Lumpur on Friday.
An expansion in household spending and sustained investment activity will drive the economy next year, with trade set to moderate, according to the central bank. A rate cut in July is working its way through the economy and will provide some support, the governor said.
The benchmark rate, at 2.75 per cent, is “supportive and appropriate” for the economy, and Malaysia has both financial and monetary room to “take further action”, if needed, he added.
Malaysia’s gross domestic product expanded 5.2 per cent in the third quarter from a year earlier, according to the central bank and statistics department. That is in line with the advance estimate and median forecast in a Bloomberg survey. On a sequential basis, the economy grew 2.4 per cent from the previous three months.
“Strong growth, which is also robust in terms of breadth, suggests that BNM will stay on hold at least through the first half of 2026,” said Sanjay Mathur, an economist with Australia & New Zealand Banking Group. “BNM can now afford to hold its firepower such that it has the capacity to respond in the event of an unanticipated shock.”


Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim likewise aired optimism earlier this week, saying the economy has the potential to exceed the government’s full-year forecast.
Policymakers are optimistic that data centres can help harness new growth opportunities for Malaysia, even as they noted that global economic conditions remain challenging. Localising the supply chain of data centres should ensure gains are spread out through the economy, the central bank said.
Data centre export services surged to 10.7 billion ringgit (US$2.6 billion) in the first nine months of the year from 1.2 billion ringgit during the same period in 2024, according to the central bank.
Next year may be more challenging. The government expects growth to moderate to 4 per cent to 4.5 per cent in 2026 amid heightened external volatility. Malaysia is still in negotiations with Washington to exempt its semiconductors from levies that could go as high as 300 per cent. The US is the Southeast Asian nation’s third-largest export market for chips.
BNM said it will continue to ensure an orderly functioning of the domestic foreign exchange market.


The ringgit has strengthened more than 8 per cent this year against the US dollar, making it the top performer in Asia. The currency held a 0.1 per cent drop as of 12.20pm local time.
“Malaysia’s positive economic growth prospects, supported by the government’s commitment to domestic structural reforms and fiscal sustainability, will continue to provide support to the ringgit,” BNM said in a statement.
Malaysia has so far shrugged off the initial impact of US President Donald Trump’s levies that took effect in August, even as neighbours Indonesia and the Philippines reported slower growth in the third quarter. Exports jumped 12.2 per cent year on year in September, surpassing all estimates, amid front-loading of electronics shipments.
Improved employment and wages supported consumption in the third quarter, while investment in both the private and public sectors continued to grow.
The central bank cut its benchmark interest rate only once in 2025. It also released more funds into the banking system in May to encourage lending and help boost economic activity.

data.title: Chinese aluminium smelter rides Hong Kong IPO wave with US$707 million fundraising drive
data.description:
Chinese aluminium smelter Chuangxin Industries Holdings aims to raise as much as HK$5.5 billion (US$707 million) in an initial public offering that has already drawn commitments from 18 domestic and international investors, highlighting the strong appetite in Hong Kong’s buoyant fundraising market.
Chuangxin planned to offer 500 million shares at HK$10.18 to HK$10.99 each, with 90 per cent allocated to institutional investors and the remainder to retail investors, according to its Hong Kong stock exchange filing on Friday. The stock is expected to start trading on November 24.
Switzerland-based commodity giant Glencore, China’s investment manager Hillhouse Group, aluminium maker China Hongqiao Group, Singapore’s commodity trading firm Mercuria Energy Group, US asset manager Millennium Management and US quant trading firm Jane Street were among the cornerstone investors that agreed to commit around US$351 million worth of stock based on the top range of the offer price.
The IPO comes amid a rebound in Hong Kong’s equities. Global investors, including US funds with long-only strategies, had rediscovered the allure of Chinese firms, some investment bankers previously said.

Total funds from new share sales soared 220 per cent in the first nine months of the year, with 66 companies raising US$23.27 billion on the Hong Kong stock exchange’s main board, according to data from the London Stock Exchange Group. That put the bourse at the top of the global IPO rankings for the first time since 2019.
More than 300 companies were in the city’s listing pipeline as international investors’ appetite returned and many Chinese firms used Hong Kong as a launch pad for their global expansion plans, Bonnie Chan Yiting, CEO of bourse operator Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing, said last month.
Chuangxin, based in China’s Inner Mongolia, planned to use half of the proceeds for expanding overseas production capacity, including a project in Saudi Arabia, according to the filing. The remainder would go towards constructing green energy projects, working capital and general corporate purposes.
The company said its Inner Mongolian smelter was the fourth-largest production base of electrolytic aluminium in north China last year.
Its revenue rose 22.6 per cent to 7.2 billion yuan (US$1 billion) in the first five months of 2025 from a year earlier, but its profit dropped 14.1 per cent to 756 million yuan during the same period owing to “the increase in market prices of certain key raw materials”.
Its biggest customer is Shanghai-listed Innovation New Material Technology, which is controlled by Chuangxin’s owner and chairman, Cui Lixin. Cui would hold 75 per cent of shares after the IPO, according to the prospectus.
China International Capital Corporation and Huatai Securities are joint sponsors of Chuangxin’s IPO.

data.title: ONE Championship: Rodtang Jitmuangnon vows to ‘fight like a rocket’ against Nong-O Hama
data.description:
Rodtang Jitmuangnon is a pure showman. From going viral for dropping his hands to marching forward with relentless pressure and fire in his gloves, he is the ultimate fan-friendly fighter. There is no “off” switch and there are no backwards steps, even when it is a friend standing across from him in the ring.
Ahead of his flyweight Muay Thai showdown with Nong-O Hama at ONE 173 in Tokyo on Sunday, the Thai superstar said he had no intention of changing his approach.
“This is my style and I cannot change who I am,” Rodtang told the Post in Tokyo. “I love to fight this way. I love to show my personality in the ring. When the opportunity arrives, I have to take it. I will do my best on Sunday.”
It is a match built on respect and history. Nong-O, now 39, is one of the most accomplished Thai fighters of his generation, while Rodtang remains the sport’s most recognisable name.
Both men have worn ONE Championship gold and inspired a new wave of athletes. For Rodtang, this rematch represents more than a belt; it is a chance to reassert himself on the world stage.

“When there is an opportunity, I must grab it,” he said. “It’s time to take my belt back.”
Despite the friendship between them, Rodtang laughed off any suggestion that he might hold back his trademark antics. “Absolutely, I’ll still be myself,” he said with a grin. “Maybe I’ll drop my hands, maybe even climb on his neck. That’s who I am.”
Asked about Nong-O’s age, Rodtang refused to take the bait. “Age is just a number,” he said.
“It’s about who has the better plan and who is better prepared. Nong-O is disciplined, he trains hard and I cannot underestimate him.”


What he believes will separate them is speed. “I will fight like a rocket,” he said. “That’s my advantage. I’ll be faster, and I’ll use that.”
Both fighters are chasing not only victory but also the performance bonuses that have become a hallmark of ONE events. “We will do our best,” Rodtang said. “Everyone wants the belt and the bonus, but in the end it’s up to Chatri, the big boss, to decide.”
Rodtang also addressed some familiar names. Japanese kickboxing star Takeru Segawa, who fought him in one of the biggest combat sports events ever held in Japan, has publicly expressed interest in a rematch.
Rodtang’s response was simple: “Just get that win. If you win, I’m ready to fight you anytime, even bare hand.”
As for Tenshin Nasukawa, who beat Rodtang in an exhibition bout in 2018 before switching to professional boxing, the Thai said there was no bad blood. “Tenshin has his goals in boxing,” Rodtang said. “If he succeeds and wants to come back to kickboxing one day, I’ll be ready to face him again.”
Beyond Tokyo, Rodtang is also excited about the rumoured return of ONE Championship to Kuala Lumpur. His appearance at fight week in traditional Malay attire earned him plenty of attention from Malaysian fans.
“I was happy to wear the Baju Melayu,” he said. “As a Muslim, I’d love to fight in Kuala Lumpur. There are many Muslim fans there, and I want to show my skills for them.”

Asked who he would want to face if that Malaysia event happens, he smiled. “Anyone,” he said. “Maybe Superlek again. I can fight anyone, anytime.”
Rodtang also shared his thoughts on Superlek’s coming fight with Yuki Yoza on the same Tokyo card. “It’s a hard fight to call,” he said. “Yuki beat Petchtanong Petchfergus and is dangerous with his boxing. I just hope Superlek doesn’t underestimate him. Don’t worry too much about the leg kicks because Yuki likes to fake and throw punches after. I’m supporting Superlek and hope he wins.”
As ever, Rodtang signed off with gratitude. “To all my fans in Thailand and around the world, please send your support,” he said. “All the Thai fighters will give everything at ONE 173. I hope everyone enjoys the show.”
